objective: To the humanitarian system deliver faster, more coherently and with greater impact for people in crisis.

howWeGetThere.text: Simplify humanitarian planning so that needs and response plans are shorter, clearer and more focused on priorities and results.
Integrate supply chains and scale common services so that delivery becomes faster, cheaper and more reliable, feeding into the wider Unified Service Roadmap for the UN system.
Strengthen humanitarian data collaboration and interoperability so that agencies can work from a common evidence base, shared beneficiary information, and better-connected data initiatives.
Bring agencies together for coordinated humanitarian diplomacy.
Clarify agency roles and strengthen joint work in food security, mobility and displacement, and health and nutrition.

progressToDate: As part of the umanitarian eset, humanitarian planning timelines have been shortened by more than 50 days, and core planning documents by 67 per cent. Pilot rollouts for integrated supply chains are under way in Afghanistan, Haiti, the Palestinian , Somalia and Sudan. A common services catalogue went live in January, with 17 service lines already available. Work is also under way on humanitarian data initiatives, including the Humanitarian Data Collaborative, beneficiary-data interoperability, public-private data collaboration and pooled funding for shared data priorities. The humanitarian diplomacy mechanism is already supporting coordinated analysis, messaging and engagement. 20 country teams have reviewed food security arrangementsjoint work on mobility and displacement is moving into implementation, and discussions continue on health and nutrition.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Simplified Humanitarian Needs and Response Plans will continue to be rolled out in 2026, with consistent application of the streamlined approach. Updated plans will be available online as they are issued.
Integrated supply chain models and common services will continue to be developed will be reflected in the Unified Services Roadmap. at this stage.

Humanitarian data initiatives will move from technical alignment into operational testing. Their concept, roadmap and financing approaches will be brought together in a Secretary-General brief. t this stage.
The humanitarian diplomacy mechanism will be further operationalized and refined across contexts.

Alignment of agency responsibilities across food security, mobility and displacement, beneficiary data, and health and nutrition will continue through 2026, including finalization of a Rome-based Agencies collaboration blueprint, pilot-country rollout and further development of joint approaches and interoperability solutions.  at this stage.

objective: To pool thematic knowledge across the UN development system to .

howWeGetThere.text: Establish Joint Knowledge Hubs that bring together analysis and expertise across UN entities on key themes, providing more joined-up policy support and eliminating duplications.

progressToDate: Over twenty development entities consulted and surveys undertaken to design the Joint Knowledge Hubs. Three pilot hubs have been selected for phase 1Trade and Regional IntegrationProductive Transformationand Strategic Foresight. For each hub, work has started on mapping the expertise and products that the hubs will bring together, agreeing co-leadership arrangements and preparing implementation plans.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Implementation plans for each pilot hub will be submitted to the Secretary-General . The hubs will be reflected in the Secretary-General’s QCPR report in May, with further details to be provided in a Secretary-General’s information brief . The pilot phase will continue until Sep, with the aim of demonstrating practical results and learning lessons for the next phase. Ton are under consideration for phase 2
No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Work will continue on the catalogue, access protocols and standard administrative . objective: To strengthen strategic oversight of the assignment of implementation responsibilities.

howWeGetThere.text: Assess senior management coordination fora and their administrative support arrangements, as part of the broader 88.
Review the wider portfolio of interagency coordination mechanisms and task forces at headquarters to reduce duplication and align them with the Regional Monthly Review platform.

progressToDate: Senior management coordination fora and their support arrangements have been mapped. Terms of reference have been prepared to review the operation of these fora, in line with A/RES/80/251. In parallel, a system-wide mapping of more than 200 other coordination mechanisms has been completed to support recommendations on rationalization and realignment.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The review of senior management coordination fora will be completed by September, as part of the broader assessment of how to strengthen internal strategic oversight, including recommendations to the Secretary-General.
Recommendations to rationalize the wider portfolio of other interagency coordination mechanisms and task forces will also be submitted to the Secretary-General by September.
No decision by an intergovernmentalis envisaged.

objective: To better use of shared core technology services, common technology expertise and modern tools.

objective: To make public UN data easier to access and use, and to build stronger joint data capacity over time.

howWeGetThere.text: Build a shared UN System Data Commons platform so that public data and statistics from different entities can be accessed in one place [data.un.org] and used reliably with AI tools.

Establish a joint programme, with shared governance, resources, teams and tools, to sustain the Data Commons and improve the coherence, quality and future readiness of system-wide data work over time.

progressToDate: An initial Data Commons platform is being developed, formally encouraged by the Statistical Commission, with 25 entities now committed to contribute data to the next phase, as well as know-how or resources. A core delivery team and seed resources have been mobilized. A joint programme proposal is in development to sustain the shared platform and deepen progress on joint standards, production workflows, data maturity and AI readiness.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: The Data Commons platform will be expanded, including the onboarding of datasets from all 25 participating entities, with a public launch in September.
The joint programme proposal will be shared for consideration by participating entities, potential partners and the Secretary-General by July. with phased implementation beginning thereafter.
No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

objective: To make core and pooled funding more attractive and effective.

howWeGetThere.text: Assess pooled funding arrangements across the UN system.
Develop recommendations to improve the portfolio of major funds.
Develop proposals to make core funding more attractive.
Develop a more coherent Funding Compact review architecture.

progressToDate: System-wide mapping and analysis have been completed across pooled funding, core funding and Funding Compact review mechanisms. This work has been supported by consultations with Member States, contributors and UN system entities. Initial findings show fragmentation across pooled funds, uneven costing and governance, structural disadvantages affecting core funding, and gaps in current Funding Compact review arrangements.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Consultations will continue through , leading to a final assessment and recommendations on better pooled funding by the end of June for consideration by the Secretary-General.
Further work and consultations with Member States will continue through May, leading to recommendations by end June for consideration by the Secretary-General.

objective: To build a leaner, more agile and field-oriented peace and security pillar, prioritizing prevention, advancing peacebuilding and political solutions and delivering joined-up impact.

howWeGetThere.text: Realign capacities currently split between DPPA and DPO to provide more coherent support to missions and country teams, by (i) establishing a unified Peacebuilding and Peace Support Office; (ii) creating a single unit on Women, Peace and Security; (iii) strengthening mechanisms to link cross-cutting functions such as data and digital innovation; (iv) consolidating two ASG posts into one; and (v) combining the West and North Africa divisions.

Optimize staffing resources and streamline support structures, by (i) integrating UNMHA into the OSESGY; (ii) streamlining and aligning UNOCA and the Office of the Special Envoy for the Great Lakes, and the Office of the Special Adviser for Cyprus with UNFICYP; and (iii) rationalizing UNRGID.

Strengthen conflict prevention capacities by hosting regional Peace and Development Advisors (PDA) in Regional Commission offices, in line with Action #48, and by integrating PDA support across relevant Resident Coordinator Offices.
Begin relocating the [offices of] SPMs with a regional focus in locations with Regional Commissions to advance cross-pillar collaboration.
Align surge capacity for peace and security, rule of law, DDR, SSR, small arms control, transitions, prevention of extremist violence, in line with Action #54.

progressToDate: The implementation of the consolidation and streamlining of key functions and positions approved by Member States in GA resolution 80/244 A-C is ongoing. In parallel, coordination and mapping exercises are advancing to strengthen capacity management and improve collaboration between offices and missions, including the integration of UNMHA into OSESGY, alignment efforts in UNFICYP and consolidation of the UNRGID leadership. Additionally, concept development and data gathering exercises have been undertaken to align surge of capacity for peace and security.

nextStepsAndDecisions.text: Implementation of A/RES/80/244 A-C is ongoing throughout 2026. No further decision by an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.
No information provided.

UNMHA 31 March 2026, with OSESGY assuming all core Hudaydah Agreement-related tasks. Residual assets, personnel arrangements and operational responsibilities will be fully transitioned to OSESGY by April 2026. No further decision by an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

Following the abolishment of the post of Special Adviser (A/RES/80/244 A-C), the proposal to rename the office as the United Nations Mission of Good Offices in Cyprus will be included in the Proposed Programme Budget for 2027 (May 2026). An updated MOU for additional support from UNFICYP is under discussion, with a view to finalization in 2026.
No information provided.
No information provided.

A comprehensive mapping of PBPSO surge capacities will be completed, which will be reflected in the Secretary-General brief in July 2026. Standing capacities on DDR, SSR, justice and corrections to continue coordinating surge deployments. No decision by an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.
No information provided.

objective: To make mandate information and related tools easier for Member States to find and use.

howWeGetThere.text: Strengthen mandates.un.org as the UN registry for mandates and improve its links with mandate registries in other entities.
Develop additional digital tools to make mandate-related information across the UN system easier to find and use.
